query: In what year was the Triple Entente formally created between Russia, France, and Britain?
What was the name of the German war plan designed to fight a two-front war by first attacking France and then Russia?
On what date was Archduke Franz Ferdinand assassinated in Sarajevo?
Who was the nineteen-year-old Bosnian Serb who shot Archduke Franz Ferdinand and his wife Sophie?
In what year did Germany and Austria-Hungary go to war, resulting in Prussia annexing several German states?
What treaty forced France to pay a large war indemnity and cede Alsace and Lorraine to Prussia after the Franco-Prussian War?
What was the name of the new class of British warship that debuted in 1906 and revolutionized naval warfare?
How many rounds per minute could the Maxim-designed machine guns of World War I fire?
What percentage of gas-related deaths in WWI were caused by phosgene gas, according to historians?
In what month and year did the British introduce tanks on the battlefield for the first time?
By 1917, approximately what percentage of the world's merchant ships had German U-boats destroyed?
How many Russian prisoners did Germany capture at the Battle of Tannenberg?
How many casualties did Austria-Hungary suffer during the siege of Przemyśl and surrounding fighting?
What was the length, in miles, of the trench system at its greatest extent on the Western Front?
On what date did Austria-Hungary declare war on Serbia, officially beginning WWI?
What was the total number of soldiers who died in WWI across all nations?
How many civilians, including women and children, were killed by German troops during the invasion of Belgium?
In what year did Japan attack and capture the German colonial port at Qingdao on the Chinese coast?
How many Chinese laborers were brought to Europe during WWI to repair equipment and dig trenches?
What was the name of the German submarine that torpedoed the RMS Lusitania in 1915?
How many U.S. citizens died when the Lusitania was sunk in May 1915?
How many ships participated in the Battle of Jutland, the only major surface naval engagement of WWI?
In what year did the United States pass the Espionage Act, limiting anti-war speech?
How many people were prosecuted under the U.S. Sedition Act of 1918?
How many years was Eugene V. Debs sentenced to prison for his anti-war comments?
What were the approximate casualties suffered by France at the Battle of Verdun (1916)?
What was the total number of casualties from both sides combined at the Battle of the Somme?
In what year did the February Revolution break out in Russia, leading to the abdication of Tsar Nicholas II?
What was the name of the new Russian national legislature created after the 1905 Revolution?
Under the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk (1918), which territories did Russia give up to the Central powers?
On what exact date and time did the WWI armistice take effect, ending the fighting?
How many casualties did Russia suffer during WWI?
How many soldiers of all nations died in WWI?
How many German soldiers died each day on the Western Front during WWI on average?
In what city was the signing of the Treaty of Versailles, which ended WWI, held?
What year did Germany withdraw from the League of Nations following Hitler's referendum?
How many British 'pals' units were devastated at the Battle of the Somme in 1916?
How many French people and Belgians had left their homes by the end of 1914 due to the war?
In what year did Britain extend the right to vote to property-owning women over thirty?
How many soldiers were in Germany's active military force at the outbreak of WWI?
What was the size of the British Expeditionary Force that arrived in France in August 1914?
How many British women volunteered for the Women's Land Army during WWI?
What were the total casualties from both sides at the Gallipoli campaign?
How many Canadian soldiers were killed at the Battle of Vimy Ridge in April 1917?
What was the size of the German force (troops, tanks, aircraft) committed to the Battle of the Bulge?
In what year did Japan detonate explosives along the South Manchuria Railway to justify invading Manchuria?
What was the name of the truce signed between China and Japan on May 22, 1933, creating a demilitarized zone?
On what date did Japanese forces enter and begin their reign of terror in Nanjing?
How many Chinese women and girls did foreign witnesses in Nanjing estimate were raped?
How many people died when Chiang Kai-shek ordered the opening of the Yellow River dikes?
How much restitution did Japan pay the United States after sinking the USS Panay gunboat?
On what date did Germany and the Soviet Union sign the German-Soviet Nonaggression Pact?
On what date did Germany invade Poland, triggering WWII?
How many troops did Germany assemble for Operation Barbarossa, the invasion of the Soviet Union?
In the first two days of Operation Barbarossa, how many Soviet planes were destroyed on the ground?
How many days did the siege of Leningrad last?
How many people perished in Leningrad during the siege, overall?
How many Allied troops were evacuated during the Dunkirk evacuation in June 1940?
On what date did Japan attack Pearl Harbor?
How many people were killed and injured in the Pearl Harbor attack combined?
How many Japanese aircraft carriers were lost at the Battle of Midway in June 1942?
In what year was the Wannsee Conference held, at which the Final Solution was decided?
How many people were sent to Auschwitz-Birkenau, and how many of them died there?
How many Jewish people and members of other minority groups were murdered in the Holocaust?
How many children did the Kindertransport rescue from Nazi Germany before the war?
In what year did the United States impose an oil and gas embargo on Japan?
How many civilian and military casualties did the United States suffer at Pearl Harbor?
What was the date of the D-Day invasion at Normandy?
By the second day of D-Day, approximately how many Allied troops had linked up in a continuous line?
On what date did Hitler commit suicide?
When was the atomic bomb first successfully tested in the Trinity Test in New Mexico?
How many people were killed instantly when the atomic bomb 'Little Boy' was dropped on Hiroshima?
How many people were killed immediately when 'Fat Man' was dropped on Nagasaki?
What were the total deaths in the Soviet Union due to WWII (military and civilian)?
How many American soldiers died in WWII?
How many forced laborers from different countries fell under German control during WWII?
How many children were evacuated from London during the German blitz?
What was the unemployment rate in the United States in 1939, and how low did it drop by 1943?
How many women served in some branch of the U.S. military during WWII?
How many Soviet women served in frontline combat positions during WWII?
How many bombing runs did the Soviet Night Witches women's air unit conduct during WWII?
In what year was the United Nations established, and in what city?
How many defendants were indicted at the Nuremberg Trials, and how many were convicted?
How long did the U.S. occupation of Japan last after WWII?
What is the 'Concert of Europe,' and what role did it play in maintaining stability in the nineteenth century?
What is a 'bloc' in international politics, and how did the European blocs form before WWI?
What is the Triple Alliance, and which countries formed it?
What is the Triple Entente, and what were its origins?
What is the Schlieffen Plan, and what was its strategic logic?
What does the term 'total war' mean, and how did WWI exemplify this concept?
What is pan-Slavic nationalism, and how did it influence events in the Balkans before WWI?
What is the 'sick man of Europe,' and why was this label applied to the Ottoman Empire?
What was the Balkan League, and who were its founding members?
What was the Black Hand organization, and what were its political aims?
What is a 'blank check' in diplomacy, and how did Germany's blank check to Austria-Hungary contribute to WWI?
What is the term 'shell shock,' and how does it relate to modern psychological diagnoses?
What is 'no-man's-land,' and what characterized this zone on the Western Front?
What is 'trench foot,' and what conditions during WWI caused it?
What are U-boats, and what role did they play in Germany's WWI naval strategy?
What is an armistice, and how did it differ from a formal surrender at the end of WWI?
What is the Sykes-Picot Agreement, and what were its main provisions?
What is the War Industries Board (WIB), and why was it significant for the U.S. war effort?
What is the Women's Land Army, and what purpose did it serve in Britain during WWI?
What is the Zimmermann Telegram, and what was Germany's offer to Mexico?
What were the Bolsheviks, and what distinguished them from other Russian socialist factions?
What is the February Revolution (1917), and what triggered it?
What is the Petrograd Soviet, and what groups did it represent?
What was the Easter Rising, and what were the goals of the Irish nationalists involved?
What is the Espionage Act, and how did it limit civil liberties in the United States during WWI?
What is the Sedition Act, and how did it differ from the Espionage Act?
What is 'isolationism,' and how did it shape U.S. foreign policy before entering WWI?
What is conscription, and which countries used it as their primary method of military recruitment during WWI?
What were 'aces' in WWI aerial combat, and why were they celebrated?
What is the concept of 'Lebensraum,' and how did it influence German foreign policy?
What was the Mukden Incident, and why did Japan use it as a pretext for invading Manchuria?
What was the Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere, and what did Japan claim it represented?
What is appeasement, and how is the Munich Pact considered the defining example of this policy?
What are the Nuremberg Laws, and how did they define and discriminate against Jewish people in Germany?
What is Kristallnacht, and what were its immediate consequences for Jewish people in Germany?
What is the Munich Analogy, and how has it been used in international diplomacy since WWII?
What is blitzkrieg, and what combination of military elements did it involve?
What was the German-Soviet Nonaggression Pact, and what secret provisions did it contain?
What is the Holocaust, and what was the scale of its destruction?
What was the Einsatzgruppen, and what was their specific function in Nazi-occupied territories?
What was the 'Final Solution,' and at what conference was it formally decided?
What is the Lend-Lease Act, and how did it allow the U.S. to support Allied nations without formally entering the war?
What was Operation Barbarossa, and what were Hitler's main strategic objectives in launching it?
What was the Atlantic Charter, and what principles did it articulate for the postwar world?
What is the concept of denazification, as applied to Germany after WWII?
What is Executive Order 9066, and what did it authorize the U.S. government to do?
What was the Manhattan Project, and at which sites was it carried out?
What is the Trinity Test, and why was it historically significant?
What is ENIAC, and what was its original military purpose?
What were 'comfort women,' and what countries' women were most victimized by Japan's system?
What was the Maginot Line, and why did it fail to protect France from Germany's invasion?
What was the Percentages Agreement, and what territories did it divide between Britain and the Soviet Union?
What was the Yalta Conference, and what were its three main outcomes?
What was the Potsdam Conference, and how did it plan to divide postwar Germany?
What was the Kindertransport, and how many children did it save?
What were the Nuremberg Trials, and which categories of crimes were prosecuted?
What was the Bataan Death March, and what conditions made it so deadly?
What was 'island-hopping,' and how did the United States use this strategy in the Pacific theater?
What is the concept of a 'war crimes trial,' as it emerged after WWII?
What was Plan D ('Europe First'), and why did U.S. military planners adopt it?
What was the Tripartite Pact, and which three countries formed the Axis under it?
What was the Warsaw Ghetto, and how many people were held there by 1941?
What was the Battle of Stalingrad, and why is it considered a turning point of WWII?
What was the 'Night and Fog Decree,' and how did it affect resistance movements in occupied Europe?
What was the Phony War, and why did very little combat occur during this period?
What is the Munich Pact, and why did Britain and France agree to it?
What was the Atlantic Squadron, and why did the United States create it in 1939?
What is the concept of 'demilitarization,' as applied to Japan after WWII?
What was the Wannsee Conference, and why is it significant in understanding the Holocaust?
What is the National Eugenics Law that Japan passed in 1940, and what did it prohibit?
What is the 'gyokusai' slogan, and how did it reflect Japanese military culture in WWII?
What is the kamikaze strategy, and what was the purpose of suicide pilots in Japan's defense?
What is the 'Quit India' movement, and who led it?
What is the Indian National Army (INA), and who assembled it during WWII?
What was the Lend-Lease Act, and which countries benefited most from it?
What was Operation Husky, and what was its strategic purpose in WWII?
What is the concept of 'unconditional surrender,' and how was it central to Allied war aims?
What is the Munich Pact's concept of appeasement, and how did it fail to prevent WWII?
What were the Neutrality Acts passed by the U.S. Congress, and what did they forbid?
What is the concept of 'denazification' as applied in the postwar Nuremberg context?
What was the Battle of Britain, and what technology gave the RAF an advantage over the Luftwaffe?
What was the Bretton Woods Conference, and what two institutions did it create?
What was the Declaration on Liberated Europe, and what did the Big Three commit to?
What is the concept of 'crimes against humanity' as it emerged from the Nuremberg and Tokyo trials?
How did the causes of WWI compare to the causes of WWII? What similarities and differences exist in the roles of nationalism, imperialism, and alliance systems?
Compare the trench warfare of WWI with the blitzkrieg strategy of WWII. How did each reflect the technology and military doctrine of its era?
How did the home front experience differ between WWI and WWII for civilians in Britain? Consider rationing, women's roles, and bombing.
Compare the roles of women in the workforce during WWI and WWII. In what ways did each conflict expand or limit women's economic participation?
How did the U.S. approach to neutrality differ between WWI and WWII? What factors eventually brought the U.S. into each conflict?
Compare the Treaty of Versailles (1919) with the postwar settlements reached at Yalta and Potsdam. How did each approach shape the subsequent peace?
How did colonial troops' roles differ between WWI and WWII? Compare the experiences of African and Asian soldiers in both conflicts.
Compare the Russian Revolution's impact on WWI with the Soviet Union's role in WWII. How did Russia's internal politics affect each war's outcome?
How did propaganda differ in effectiveness and reach between WWI and WWII? Compare the methods used by Germany, the United States, and Britain.
Compare the naval strategies of Germany in WWI and WWII. How did U-boat warfare evolve between the two conflicts?
Compare the Eastern Front and Western Front of WWI. How did geography, tactics, and outcomes differ between the two theaters?
How did the experiences of soldiers in the trenches of France differ from those fighting in the mountains of the Alps or the deserts of the Middle East during WWI?
Compare Germany's Schlieffen Plan with France's Plan XVII. What assumptions did each make, and how did each perform in practice?
How did Britain's volunteer military system compare with Germany's conscription-based army at the outset of WWI in terms of size and readiness?
Compare the military contributions of ANZAC forces at Gallipoli with Canadian forces at Vimy Ridge. How did each campaign shape national identity?
How did the Ottoman Empire's collapse compare with Austria-Hungary's disintegration as contributing factors to WWI?
Compare Germany's treatment of civilians in Belgium during WWI with Germany's treatment of civilians in occupied Europe during WWII.
How did Russia's capacity to fight in WWI compare to its capacity under Soviet leadership in WWII?
Compare the socialists' response to WWI in France, Germany, and Russia. How did each socialist movement navigate the tension between internationalism and nationalism?
How did the armistice of November 1918 differ from Germany's unconditional surrender in May 1945? What were the political consequences of each?
Compare Japan's motivations for expansion in Asia with Germany's motivations for expansion in Europe during the 1930s and 1940s.
How did Hitler's appeasement at Munich in 1938 compare with Japan's diplomatic dealings with the West before Pearl Harbor?
Compare the Holocaust's implementation in Poland with the genocide of Armenians during WWI. What similarities and differences exist in methods and scale?
How did the Battle of Stalingrad compare to the Battle of Midway as turning points in the European and Pacific theaters, respectively?
Compare the Allied strategy of 'Europe First' with the simultaneous operations in the Pacific. How were resources allocated between the two theaters?
How did the Japanese occupation of Southeast Asia differ from European colonial rule in the same region? What promises did Japan make and fail to keep?
Compare Mussolini's Italy with Hitler's Germany as fascist regimes. How did each come to power, and how did each fare militarily?
How did resistance movements in Yugoslavia under Tito compare to the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising in terms of organization, leadership, and outcome?
Compare the internment of Japanese Americans with the treatment of Jewish people under the Nuremberg Laws. What are the key similarities and differences?
How did the Yalta Conference agreements compare to the Potsdam Conference agreements regarding the division of Europe?
Compare Germany's four-year economic plan with the Soviet Union's centrally commanded economy in terms of how each nation prepared for and sustained war production.
How did air warfare in the Battle of Britain compare to the Allied strategic bombing of Germany? What were the goals and results of each campaign?
Compare the scientific contributions of the Manhattan Project with Britain's code-breaking efforts at Bletchley Park. How did each contribute to Allied victory?
How did Japan's wartime treatment of women (comfort women, motherhood incentives) compare with Germany's Nazi policies toward women?
Compare Soviet women's frontline combat roles with U.S. women's industrial roles ('Rosie the Riveter') during WWII.
How did the role of the League of Nations after WWI compare to the role of the United Nations established after WWII?
Compare Germany's 'war guilt' under the Treaty of Versailles with Japan's reckoning under the Tokyo War Crimes Trials.
How did post-WWI Germany's economic suffering under the Versailles reparations compare to post-WWII Germany's recovery under Allied occupation?
Compare the civilian death tolls of WWI with those of WWII. What accounts for the dramatic increase in civilian casualties in the second conflict?
How did the use of new technology in WWI (tanks, poison gas, submarines) compare to WWII technological innovations (atomic bomb, radar, computers)?
Compare the strategic roles of the United States in WWI and WWII. How did late entry affect the war's outcome in each case?
How did the experience of Jewish communities in WWI-era Europe compare to their experience under Nazism in the 1930s and WWII?
Compare the Bolshevik Revolution in Russia (1917) with the rise of fascism in Italy and Germany in the 1920s and 1930s. How did each fill the power vacuum left by WWI?
How did the experiences of colonial peoples during WWI compare to those during WWII? Did either war hasten decolonization?
Compare Germany's military successes in the early stages of both WWI and WWII. Why was Germany initially effective in both conflicts, and what ultimately led to defeat in each?
How did the Zimmermann Telegram (WWI) compare to Germany's diplomatic overtures to Japan before WWII as attempts to distract or neutralize the United States?
Compare the Gallipoli campaign of WWI with the Normandy D-Day invasion of WWII in terms of strategic goals, planning, and outcome.
How did rationing policies in Britain during WWI compare to those implemented during WWII? What was rationed, and how severe were shortages in each war?
Compare the psychological impact of WWI ('shell shock') on soldiers with the broader civilian psychological trauma documented in WWII bombing campaigns.
How did the use of poison gas in WWI compare to Germany's use of gas in Holocaust death camps during WWII in terms of scale, intent, and moral legacy?
Compare the experiences of African American soldiers in WWI's segregated units with the experiences of Black soldiers in WWII. Did conditions improve?
How did the collapse of Russia's tsarist government (WWI) compare to the collapse of Japan's military government structure following WWII?
Compare the economic mobilization of the United States in WWI versus WWII. Which was more extensive, and what new institutions were created in each?
How did Germany's 'blank check' to Austria-Hungary (WWI) compare to the Tripartite Pact (WWII) as instruments of military alliance?
Compare the role of Australia and New Zealand in WWI (Gallipoli, Western Front) with their roles in WWII (Pacific Theater).
How did the Middle East as a theater of war in WWI compare to its role in WWII? What resources were at stake in each conflict?
Compare the Irish Easter Rising (1916) with Indian resistance to British rule during WWII. How did each challenge colonial authority during wartime?
How did Germany's submarine warfare differ in WWI versus WWII in terms of targets, restrictions, and impact on neutral nations?
Compare France's defeat and occupation in WWII with its role as a major fighting power in WWI. What accounts for the dramatic difference in resistance capacity?
How did the Final Solution (WWII) compare with the Armenian Genocide (WWI) in terms of state organization, scale, and international response?
Compare the Sykes-Picot Agreement (WWI) with the Atlantic Charter (WWII) as frameworks for determining the postwar international order.
How did the role of Italy differ in WWI versus WWII? Compare its alliances, motivations, and military performance in each conflict.
Compare the experience of Japanese Americans interned during WWII with the treatment of German Americans suspected of disloyalty during WWI.
How did the war's effect on women's suffrage differ between WWI (leading to votes for women in 1918–1920) and WWII? Did the second war advance or retard women's political rights?
Compare the leadership styles and war aims of Woodrow Wilson (WWI) and Franklin D. Roosevelt (WWII) in bringing the United States into global conflict.
How did the Paris Peace Conference of 1919 compare to the Potsdam and Yalta Conferences of 1945 in terms of fairness to defeated nations?
Compare the moral justifications offered by Germany for aggression in WWI with those offered by Nazi Germany in WWII.
How did the treatment of prisoners of war differ between the Western Front (WWI) and the Pacific Theater (WWII)?
Compare the scope of destruction in French and Belgian cities during WWI with the Allied bombing of German and Japanese cities in WWII.
How did the role of India in supporting British war efforts differ between WWI and WWII? Compare contributions, expectations, and political consequences.
Compare the role of air power in WWI (reconnaissance, dogfights) with its decisive role in WWII (strategic bombing, Pearl Harbor, Battle of Britain).
How did Lenin's peace campaign ('Peace, Land, Bread') during WWI compare to Gandhi's 'Quit India' movement during WWII as challenges to imperial powers?
Compare Germany's military strategies on the Eastern Front in both WWI and WWII. How did the Russian winter affect operations in each conflict?
How did the collapse of the Ottoman Empire after WWI compare to Japan's postwar reconstruction under American occupation? What were the different outcomes?
Compare civilian resistance in occupied France during WWII with civilian opposition to the war in Germany and Britain during WWI.
How did Japan's actions in China (1931–1945) compare to Germany's actions in Poland (1939–1945) in terms of occupation policies, atrocities, and resistance?
Compare Germany's wartime economic management under the four-year plan with the United States' War Industries Board (WWI) and War Production Board (WWII).
How did the British naval blockade of Germany in WWI compare to the American naval campaign against Japan in WWII? What were the effects on civilian populations?
Compare the roles of chemical weapons in WWI with the role of nuclear weapons in ending WWII. How did each weapon transform warfare and international law?
How did the 'Rape of Belgium' in WWI compare to the 'Rape of Nanjing' in WWII as events that shaped international perceptions of an aggressor nation?
Compare the political consequences of the armistice of 1918 for Germany with the consequences of Japan's unconditional surrender in 1945.
How did the propaganda efforts of the United States in WWI (Committee on Public Information) compare to those in WWII in terms of methods and effectiveness?
Compare the fates of ethnic and religious minorities in the Ottoman Empire during WWI with the fates of minorities under Nazi rule in WWII.
How did the postwar reconstruction of Japan under U.S. occupation compare to the reconstruction of Germany under Allied occupation in terms of speed, scope, and long-term outcomes?

How did the interlocking alliance systems, imperial rivalries, arms races, and nationalist movements collectively create the conditions that made a single assassination capable of triggering a world war in 1914?

In what ways did the military, economic, and political failures of WWI collectively produce the conditions that made the rise of fascism in Germany, Italy, and Japan not only possible but arguably inevitable?
How did racial ideologies, colonial hierarchies, and the logic of total war interact to shape the treatment of colonial soldiers, prisoners of war, and civilian minorities in both WWI and WWII?
Synthesize the roles of geography, logistics, technology, and political leadership in determining why Germany ultimately lost both WWI and WWII despite early military successes.

How did the experiences of industrial workers, women, colonial subjects, and political dissidents on the home front during WWI reshape postwar political movements, suffrage campaigns, and calls for independence?
How did the failure of the League of Nations, the Great Depression, the rise of fascism, and the policy of appeasement interact to make another world war nearly unavoidable by the late 1930s?
Analyze how religion, ethnicity, nationalism, and imperial competition in the Balkans between 1908 and 1914 transformed a regional conflict into a global catastrophe.

How did technological innovation in the fields of weaponry, communications, medicine, and transportation simultaneously increase the destructive capacity of WWI while also creating new forms of care and survival for soldiers?

Synthesize the political, military, and humanitarian consequences of the Sykes-Picot Agreement, the Balfour Declaration, and the Arab Revolt to explain the roots of ongoing conflicts in the Middle East.
How did the intersection of colonialism, racism, and wartime military strategy determine which populations bore the heaviest burdens of labor and death in both WWI and WWII?
How did the February Revolution, the October Revolution, and the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk together transform the trajectory of WWI in ways that shaped the global political order for decades?
Analyze how the combination of new military technologies (machine guns, gas, artillery, submarines, aircraft) and outdated military tactics produced the horrific casualties characteristic of WWI.

How did the economic, political, and cultural effects of WWI's home front—rationing, propaganda, censorship, women's labor, and political radicalism—lay the groundwork for the interwar crises of the 1920s?
Using evidence from military campaigns in France, the Caucasus, the Alps, and the Middle East, explain how geography fundamentally shaped the nature and outcome of WWI across different theaters.
How did the pan-Slavic movement, Austrian imperial ambitions, and Ottoman collapse interact with great-power politics to make the Balkans the most explosive region in pre-WWI Europe?
Synthesize the military, diplomatic, and psychological consequences of Germany's 'blank check' to Austria-Hungary in July 1914 for the subsequent escalation of a regional crisis into a world war.
How did the WWI experiences of Canada, Australia, New Zealand, and India simultaneously strengthen imperial bonds and plant the seeds of future independence movements?
Analyze how the combination of German U-boat warfare, the sinking of the Lusitania, the Sussex Pledge, and the Zimmermann Telegram created an irresistible political pressure for U.S. entry into WWI.

How did the interplay of socialist ideology, nationalist sentiment, and wartime hardship produce different political outcomes in Russia (Bolshevik Revolution), Germany (Weimar Republic), and Italy (fascism) after WWI?
Synthesize the role of propaganda, censorship, and public opinion in shaping—and distorting—civilian understanding of WWI in Germany, Britain, and the United States.

How did Japan's perception of Western racism, the rejection of its racial equality amendment at Versailles, and its desire for natural resources interact to produce the expansionist ideology behind the Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere?

Analyze how the Holocaust required the cooperation of military forces, civilian bureaucracies, railway systems, and ordinary citizens across multiple countries, and what this reveals about the role of institutions in enabling genocide.

How did the Manhattan Project bring together refugee scientists from Nazi Europe, U.S. industrial capacity, and government secrecy to produce the atomic bomb, and what moral dilemmas did this creation pose?
Synthesize the strategic, moral, and political arguments for and against Truman's decision to drop atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki, drawing on evidence from the text's primary sources.

How did Germany's invasion of the Soviet Union, the siege of Leningrad, and the Battle of Stalingrad collectively reshape the entire strategic balance of WWII in Europe, and what role did logistics play?

Analyze how Operation Barbarossa, the German four-year economic plan, and the Soviet relocation of factories east of the Urals together illustrate the critical importance of industrial production in determining WWII's outcome.
How did the British policy of appeasement, the Munich Pact, and the subsequent occupation of Czechoslovakia collectively undermine international order and embolden Hitler to launch WWII?

Synthesize the roles of propaganda, cultural suppression, economic exploitation, and military violence in Japan's administration of the Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere, and explain why resistance movements emerged.

How did the Yalta Conference decisions regarding Poland, Germany's occupation zones, and Soviet participation in the Pacific war simultaneously reflect Allied unity and plant the seeds of Cold War tensions?

Analyze how the experiences of African soldiers fighting for European colonial powers during WWII, combined with wartime promises of self-determination in the Atlantic Charter, accelerated African decolonization after the war.

How did the experiences of the two world wars together reshape the concept of international law, from the creation of war crimes tribunals to the prohibition of genocide and the founding of the United Nations?
Synthesize the ways in which WWI's unresolved issues—the 'war guilt' clause, reparations, territorial changes, and thwarted nationalisms—directly produced the political instability that enabled WWII.

How did advances in radar technology, code-breaking (Enigma), computing (ENIAC), and medical science (penicillin, blood plasma) during WWII collectively transform both military capability and postwar civilian life?
Analyze the relationship between economic depression, political extremism, and military aggression in the 1930s by synthesizing events in Germany, Japan, and Italy.

How did the treatment of women—from their mobilization in factories and the military to policies controlling their reproductive roles—reveal the contradictions between wartime necessity and patriarchal ideology in WWII belligerent nations?

Synthesize how the combination of total war, strategic bombing, and the atomic bomb transformed the relationship between civilians and warfare, blurring the line between combatants and non-combatants.

How did the experiences of Jewish refugees attempting to flee Nazi persecution—through the Kindertransport, the MS St. Louis voyage, and the search for safe havens in Shanghai and elsewhere—illustrate the failures of the international community in the late 1930s?
Analyze the role of racial ideology in shaping military policies and atrocities in WWI (Armenian Genocide, African porter deaths) and WWII (Holocaust, Japanese internment, comfort women).

How did the postwar occupations of Germany and Japan by the Allies pursue demilitarization, democratization, and economic rebuilding simultaneously, and which country's reconstruction proved more durable?

Synthesize how the experiences of colonial soldiers from Africa, India, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific in both world wars both reinforced and challenged imperial relationships between colonizers and colonized.
How did the interplay of military setbacks, food shortages, civilian unrest, and political radicalism produce revolutionary change in Russia during WWI and contribute to Germany's collapse in 1918?

Analyze how the atomic bombing of Japan, the revelations of the Holocaust, and the formation of the United Nations together marked a fundamental transformation in global ethics, international law, and the nature of sovereignty after 1945.

How did the combination of geographical isolation, economic self-interest, political isolationism, and moral outrage shape the U.S. public's gradual shift from neutrality to belligerence in both world wars?

Synthesize the ways in which WWI and WWII together permanently altered the global balance of power, dismantling the European empires and enabling the rise of the United States and the Soviet Union as superpowers.

How did the interaction of anti-Semitic propaganda, legal discrimination (Nuremberg Laws), Kristallnacht, and the Wannsee Conference represent a systematic escalation that ultimately produced the Final Solution?

Analyze how the experiences of Indian soldiers and civilians during WWI and WWII, combined with British wartime promises and broken commitments, shaped the Indian independence movement that culminated in 1947.

Synthesize the military, logistical, and political factors that determined the success of D-Day (June 6, 1944), drawing on the planning process, the deception campaign, and the coordination of Allied forces.

How did the combination of Japan's 'gyokusai' culture, kamikaze tactics, and civilian suicide at Okinawa inform U.S. estimates of invasion casualties, and how did this reasoning shape Truman's decision to use the atomic bomb?

Analyze how the collapse of the Ottoman Empire, the Sykes-Picot Agreement, and the Balfour Declaration together created a political landscape in the Middle East that generated conflicts persisting into the twenty-first century.

How did the experiences of African Americans during WWI—in segregated military units, labor units, and on the home front—compare with their expanded but still-limited roles in WWII, and what does this reveal about the limits of wartime progress on racial equality?

Synthesize the technological, strategic, and human dimensions of the Battle of Britain to explain why Hitler ultimately failed to subdue Britain in 1940 and what consequences this failure had for the outcome of WWII.
How did the Neutrality Acts, the Lend-Lease Act, and the Atlantic Charter together chart the evolution of U.S. foreign policy from isolationism to global engagement in the period 1935–1941?

Analyze how the combination of forced labor, resource extraction, rationing, and economic disruption in German-occupied Europe illustrated the full weight of total war on civilian populations beyond the battlefield.
How did the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor simultaneously transform the United States' domestic politics, its military posture, and its relationship with its Asian and European allies in WWII?

Synthesize the roles of Charles de Gaulle's Free France, partisan movements in Yugoslavia, the Soviet Red Army, and Allied landings in Italy in demonstrating how resistance and coalition warfare gradually turned the tide against Germany.

How did the failure of the League of Nations—due to U.S. non-participation, the absence of enforcement mechanisms, and the rise of aggressive revisionist states—offer lessons that directly shaped the design of the United Nations?
Analyze how the interplay of military defeat, food shortages, and domestic unrest in Germany in 1917–1918 mirrors—and differs from—the same dynamics in Japan in 1944–1945.

How did the post-WWII Nuremberg and Tokyo trials pioneer new concepts in international law—including crimes against humanity and command responsibility—that transformed global governance in subsequent decades?
Synthesize how the decisions made at the Tehran, Yalta, and Potsdam conferences collectively laid the foundations for both the Cold War division of Europe and the postwar international order.

How did the combination of scientific innovation, industrial mobilization, and strategic bombing in WWII reveal both the extraordinary capacity and the profound ethical dangers of harnessing national science and industry for warfare?

Analyze how the wartime experience of rationing, industrial mobilization, and women's labor in the Soviet Union, the United States, and Britain both reflected and challenged existing social hierarchies.

How did Japan's colonial policies in Korea, Manchuria, and Southeast Asia—including comfort women, forced labor, and puppet regimes—contradict its stated goal of 'liberating Asia from Western colonialism'?

Synthesize the ways in which the two world wars, despite their devastating costs, accelerated social change for women—in terms of voting rights, workforce participation, and political identity—in Europe and North America.

How did the German policy of collective reprisals against civilian populations in occupied Europe (e.g., the Kragujevac massacre, Night and Fog Decree) interact with partisan resistance movements in a cycle of violence?

Analyze how the Allied powers' wartime alliances—particularly the Anglo-American 'special relationship' and the uneasy alliance with Stalin's Soviet Union—both enabled Allied victory and sowed the seeds of postwar rivalry.

How did the experiences of neutral nations (Switzerland, Sweden, Ireland, Spain, Portugal) during WWI and WWII reveal the complex economic, political, and moral tensions of maintaining neutrality during total war?

Synthesize the relationship between the rise of totalitarianism in the 1920s and 1930s and the breakdown of the Versailles system, explaining how ideology, economic grievance, and strategic opportunism interacted.

How did the wartime mobilization of science and technology—from the Enigma machine to the Manhattan Project—reshape the relationship between governments, universities, and the private sector in ways that outlasted the war?

Analyze the ways in which the Jewish experience during WWII—from legal discrimination and forced emigration to the ghettos and death camps—illustrates the escalating logic of genocide when not effectively challenged.

How did the intersection of indigenous nationalism, Japanese occupation, and colonial rule in India, Vietnam, Burma, and the Philippines during WWII create new political movements that would reshape Asia after 1945?

Synthesize how the experiences of WWII soldiers, sailors, and airmen from Australia, New Zealand, Canada, South Africa, and India simultaneously reinforced imperial loyalty and accelerated the political processes leading to full independence.

How did the combined effect of the Armenian Genocide (WWI), the Holocaust (WWII), and other mass atrocities of the twentieth century eventually produce the 1948 UN Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide?
